backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The anterior compartment of the arm contains the chief flexors: biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis, all supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C7).

Clinical Pearl: A mid-shaft humerus fracture can injure the radial nerve in the spiral groove posteriorly, causing wrist drop — but the anterior compartment muscles are spared.

Memory Aid: "BBC flexes the arm" — Biceps, Brachialis, Coracobrachialis.

The posterior compartment houses the triceps brachii (long, lateral, and medial heads), the sole extensor of the elbow, supplied by the radial nerve (C6-C8).

Clinical Pearl: Compartment syndrome of the arm is rare but can follow supracondylar fractures.

Memory Aid: "The radial nerve TRIes to extend" — radial nerve supplies triceps.

The anterior forearm compartment contains flexors and pronators in superficial, intermediate, and deep layers. Key muscles include FCR, palmaris longus, FDS, and FDP.

Clinical Pearl: Volkmann's ischaemic contracture results from untreated anterior forearm compartment syndrome.

Memory Aid: "Median is the MAIN nerve of the front of the forearm."

The posterior forearm compartment contains the extensors of the wrist and digits, all supplied by the posterior interosseous nerve.

Clinical Pearl: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) involves the common extensor origin.

Memory Aid: "PIN extends the digits" — Posterior Interosseous Nerve powers all digital extensors.

The palmar aponeurosis is a thick triangular sheet of deep fascia in the palm, continuous with the palmaris longus tendon.

Clinical Pearl: Dupuytren's contracture is fibrosis of the palmar aponeurosis, causing progressive flexion of ring and little fingers.

Memory Aid: "Dupuytren's Drinks and Diabetics" — the 3 D's of risk factors.
